“A friend asked if I could help organize the PDF notes from an online course they had access to. There wasn’t a download button, so I assumed the PDFs were protected. A few minutes inside Chrome DevTools proved otherwise.”
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A friend of mine, who comes from a commerce background, had access to an online course with dozens of PDF notes.
There was just one problem.
The platform let them read every document directly in the browser, but there was no download button. They wanted offline copies to organize, annotate, and study more comfortably, but downloading each document didn’t seem possible through the interface.
My first assumption was that the PDFs were genuinely protected. Maybe the viewer streamed individual pages. Maybe it rendered images instead of the original document. Maybe the server never exposed the PDF file at all.
A few minutes inside Chrome DevTools completely changed that assumption.
What followed became one of my favorite debugging sessions — not because the problem was especially difficult, but because it reinforced an engineering lesson I keep running into:
Before trying to work around software, understand how it actually works.

The Investigation
Like most investigations, this one started with curiosity.
The course displayed every document inside a browser-based PDF viewer. The interface had zoom controls, page navigation, and search — but noticeably lacked a Download button.
Instead of guessing, I opened Chrome DevTools.
The first stop was the Network tab.
After refreshing the page, one request immediately stood out.
It returned:
200 OK
Content-Type: application/pdfThat told me everything I needed to know.
The browser wasn’t receiving images.
It wasn’t reconstructing pages.
It wasn’t streaming tiny chunks.
It was downloading the entire PDF file.
The file already existed inside the browser.
The missing download button wasn’t evidence that the document was protected — it was simply absent from the user interface.

The Temporary URL Trap
Naturally, I copied the PDF URL from the Network tab and opened it in another tab.
It failed immediately.
The request returned an authorization error.
At first that seemed contradictory.
If the browser had already downloaded the file, why couldn’t I access the same URL?
The answer turned out to be surprisingly common.
The platform generated temporary, tokenized URLs tied to the authenticated browser session. Those links expired quickly and weren’t intended to be reused.
The URL wasn’t the important part.
The authenticated browser session was.
That realization completely changed my approach.
Instead of recreating network requests manually, I started asking a different question:
What is the browser already capable of doing?
That question turned out to be the breakthrough.

Digging into PDF.js
While inspecting the page, I noticed the document wasn’t rendered directly — it lived inside an <iframe>.
The viewer itself looked familiar.
It turned out to be a customized implementation of Mozilla’s PDF.js, the open-source PDF viewer used by countless websites.
That was a huge clue.
Open-source software usually exposes well-structured APIs internally.
Chrome DevTools lets you change the JavaScript execution context, so I switched from the main page into the iframe hosting the PDF viewer and began exploring the global objects.
One immediately stood out:
window.PDFViewerApplicationThis object represents the running PDF.js application.
It contains the viewer state, rendering information, document metadata, and various helper methods.
Then I noticed something even more interesting.
window.PDFViewerApplication.download();I executed it.
Immediately, the browser’s native download dialog appeared.
That single method invoked the exact download workflow the toolbar button would normally call.
The website hadn’t removed the download functionality.
It had only removed the button that triggered it.
That was the turning point.
The challenge was no longer reverse-engineering PDF downloads.
It was simply understanding how the existing software had been designed.

Solving One Download Wasn’t the Real Problem
Downloading one PDF was satisfying.
Unfortunately, there weren’t just one or two documents.
The course library contained dozens of PDFs accumulated over multiple sessions, and the files weren’t organized in a way that made studying easy.
Questions and solutions were mixed together.
Older and newer sessions were interleaved.
My friend wanted something much cleaner:

• Session 1 — Questions

• Session 1 — Solutions

• Session 2 — Questions

• Session 2 — Solutions
Downloading each file manually wasn’t difficult anymore.
It was simply repetitive.
Whenever I find myself performing the same browser actions dozens of times, I start thinking about automation.
Not because the task is impossible — but because software is very good at repetitive work.

Building the Automation
Rather than writing a fragile login script to deal with authentication, SSO, or CAPTCHAs, I took a simpler approach.
I reused the existing authenticated Chrome session.
By launching Chrome with remote debugging enabled and attaching Playwright to it, the automation inherited the active cookies, authentication tokens, and session automatically.
The browser already knew how to access every document.
The script simply orchestrated what a user would otherwise have done manually.
At a high level, it:

• Connected to the existing browser session

• Collected every document link

• Parsed filenames to determine session order

• Sorted them into a logical reading sequence

• Opened each viewer

• Waited until PDF.js finished loading

• Invoked the built-in download method

• Repeated the process
One small detail required a little patience.
Because the PDF loads asynchronously, the script couldn’t immediately call the download method.
It first waited until PDFViewerApplication reported that the document had been fully loaded into memory.
Only then did it execute:
window.PDFViewerApplication.download();Sometimes the most effective automation isn’t about replacing software.
It’s simply asking the software to perform the action it already knows how to perform.

A Couple of Unexpected Engineering Lessons
Like many automation projects, the interesting parts weren’t the obvious ones.
A few implementation details ended up consuming more time than expected.
For example, once Chrome managed downloads directly, my automation framework no longer tracked those downloads the way I initially expected. Watching the local filesystem turned out to be a far more reliable way of determining when a file had finished downloading.
Windows also introduced a small surprise.
Even after a download appeared complete, attempting to rename or move the file immediately would occasionally fail because another process still held a temporary lock on it.
A short retry loop solved the problem reliably.
Neither issue had anything to do with PDFs.
They were reminders that real-world automation often depends just as much on understanding browser behavior and operating system quirks as it does on writing code.

What This Project Taught Me
Looking back, none of the individual techniques were especially complicated.
The valuable part was the investigation.
A few lessons stood out.
DevTools reveals far more than most developers realize.
Many “mysteries” disappear once you inspect network requests, execution contexts, and browser state.
Always investigate what the browser already has.
If a web application is successfully displaying information, chances are the browser already possesses far more data than you might assume.
The simplest solution often already exists.
I initially imagined building custom download logic.
Instead, the application already exposed the exact method I needed.
Understanding architecture beats brute force.
Recognizing PDF.js and understanding how it fit into the page saved hours of unnecessary reverse engineering.
The difficult part wasn’t writing code.
It was asking the right questions.

Final Thoughts
One of my favorite aspects of software engineering is that debugging often feels like detective work.
You begin with assumptions.
Most of them turn out to be wrong.
Each observation leads to a better question, and eventually those questions reveal how the system actually works.
In this case, the missing download button wasn’t really the story.
The story was understanding the application’s architecture instead of fighting it, discovering that the browser already knew exactly how to perform the task, and then building a small automation around that existing capability to help organize a large collection of legitimately accessible study material.
Sometimes the best engineering isn’t about writing clever code.
It’s about realizing the software already knows the answer — you just have to find where it’s hiding.
